text_no:
Takk, kjære. Takk, lille venn. Er ikke det nydelig? La oss si "Pris Herren" for den lille jenta. Gud velsigne deg, kjære. Fantastisk, fantastisk. Gud velsigne deg. Se på henne. Vel, velsign hennes lille hjerte. [En søster snakker til Bror Branham.] Hva... Gud velsigne deg. Vel, Gud velsigne hennes lille hjerte.
Jeg vil dele dette vitnesbyrdet nå. Den lille jenta kunne verken snakke eller gjøre noe da hun kom for fire år siden. Hun brakte dette som en liten erindring om helbredelsen, da hun ble helbredet for fire år siden. La oss alle si, "Pris Herren".

text:
Going to get well? [The sister continues talking to Brother Branham.] What? What was the matter with him? Her husband was healed a few nights ago. Said he was setting near the post there and was called out with cancer. And he's healed. There he is standing in the aisle back there. Let's say, "Praise the Lord." [unclear words]. Let's say, "Praise the Lord" for that. How wonderful. Her husband. That's fine. Said he come up from down around Douglas, or somewhere, that the--from Brother King's church. All right.
We're thankful to hear of them. That's mighty nice, a little commemoration to come back just to give it me. I'm… A little Spanish girl. She was awfully afflicted, and she couldn't speak. And her little hands was just growed together, or something another, as little stubs then. I believe the child will be all right now.
text_no:
Føler han seg bedre? [Søsteren fortsetter å snakke med Bror Branham.] Hva? Hva var galt med ham? Hennes ektemann ble helbredet for noen netter siden. Han sa at han satt nær stolpen der og ble kalt frem med kreft. Og han er helbredet. Der står han i midtgangen der bak. La oss si: "Pris Herren." [uklare ord]. La oss si: "Pris Herren" for det. Hvor vidunderlig. Hennes ektemann. Det er fint. Han sa han kom fra Douglas-området, eller et sted der nede, fra Bror Kings menighet. Greit.
Vi er takknemlige for å høre om dem. Det er veldig hyggelig, en liten påminnelse om å komme tilbake bare for å fortelle det til meg. Jeg… En liten spansk jente. Hun var veldig plaget, og hun kunne ikke snakke. Og hennes små hender var bare vokst sammen, eller noe lignende, som små stubber. Jeg tror barnet vil bli helt bra nå.

text:
Now, back to the life story, of when we were in the… That night, the girl, when they turned the light on me and let--made me walk up the road. And I went up and set down in a field and cried. And I was ready to try to take my life. I said, "Oh, I just don't know. I'm ready to end this thing." I said, "How in the world could I ever go through life and everybody against me?" Looked like when I'd go home, they'd have parties and so forth. Then when I would--when I would try to get out with people, I was misunderstood. I never was understood right until I got amongst this group of people. That's exactly right. Then I had people who understood me and loved me.
And then on down, some of you might wondering, being so backward and bashful, how I ever come to get married. I'll tell you about it as quick as I can.
text_no:
Tilbake til min livshistorie. Den natten, da jenta... Da de lyste på meg og fikk meg til å gå opp veien. Jeg gikk oppover og satte meg i en åker og gråt. Jeg var klar til å ende livet mitt. Jeg sa, "Åh, jeg orker ikke mer. Jeg er klar til å avslutte dette." Jeg tenkte, "Hvordan i all verden skal jeg klare å leve videre når alle er imot meg?" Det virket som om de hadde fester hjemme når jeg ikke var der. Og når jeg prøvde å være sammen med andre, ble jeg misforstått. Jeg følte meg aldri riktig forstått før jeg kom blant denne gruppen av mennesker. Det er helt riktig. Da møtte jeg mennesker som forsto og elsket meg.
Noen av dere lurer kanskje på hvordan jeg, så tilbaketrukket og sjenert, noen gang fikk meg til å gifte meg. Jeg skal fortelle om det så raskt jeg kan.

text:
Oh, my. That… After that girl done me like that, I just soured myself against the women. I said, "I didn't have nothing to do with them at all." And I thought that was horrible. I said, "I'll never have nothing to do with any more girls. I'll never go out with one as long as I live."
I'd go down the street, and I'd see one on one side of the street, I'd cross over and get on the other, if I thought she was going to speak to me. I was really against it.
text_no:
Åh, etter at den jenta behandlet meg slik, fikk jeg en bitter holdning mot kvinner. Jeg sa: "Jeg skal aldri ha noe med dem å gjøre igjen." Jeg syntes det var fryktelig. Jeg sa: "Jeg skal aldri mer ha noe med noen jenter å gjøre. Jeg skal aldri gå ut med en igjen så lenge jeg lever."
Når jeg gikk nedover gaten og så en jente på den ene siden, krysset jeg over til den andre siden hvis jeg trodde hun ville snakke til meg. Jeg var virkelig imot det.

text:
So, one day I happened to be out making a high-dive somewhere, out of a tree. And a car drove up, and a young lady stepped out. And there it was again. So there it all started. She happened to be a Christian girl, my little boy's mother.
And she started me going to church. And I went with her for about six or--six or eight months. And she was such a nice girl, so friendly, and nice, and ladylike. That's the type of girl that I liked. Only her father was… Well, he done pretty well. He had a good job, making about five hundred and something dollars a month on Pennsylvania … organizer on the Pennsylvania Railroad. I made twenty cents a hour. He drove a Buick, and I had an old T-Model Ford, backslid. So I… Quite a difference in the way we had to live.
text_no:
En dag hoppet jeg fra et høyt tre, og en bil kom kjørende opp. En ung dame steg ut av bilen, og der var hun igjen. Slik startet det hele. Hun var en kristen jente, moren til min lille gutt.
Hun begynte å ta meg med til menigheten, og jeg fulgte med henne i seks til åtte måneder. Hun var en svært hyggelig jente—vennlig, dannet og veldig kvinnelig—akkurat den type jente jeg likte. Hennes far hadde en god jobb som organisator for Pennsylvania Railroad, og tjente over fem hundre dollar i måneden. Jeg tjente tjue cent i timen. Han kjørte en Buick, mens jeg hadde en gammel T-modell Ford i dårlig forfatning. Det var en betydelig forskjell i våre livsstiler.

text:
So I liked her, and I went with her. So I remember… I know I had to either marry her, or ask her if she would marry me, or let somebody go. She was too good a girl just to take up her time like that. She'd make somebody a good wife. So I didn't… I wanted to be… I loved her well enough, that I didn't want to ruin her life like that.
So I said, "I got to make up my mind now, and I haven't got the nerve to ask her." So I said, "Now, what can I do?"
So I guess you wonder how I ever asked her. Well, I tried to ask her. And you know how that great big lump comes up in your throat here, and you can't swallow, you know, when you're trying to say anything? I'd say every time I'd go… "Now, I'm going to ask her tonight. Yes, sir, I'll do it." And I'd be telling her say, "Now, ten minutes more by my watch, I'll ask her." I'd say. Then she'd roll those eyes, didn't do any good. I couldn't ask her.
So guess you wonder how I ever got married. I wrote her a letter and asked her. Yes. I wrote her a letter, and I… Now, it wasn't a "Dear Miss…" It had a little more mush, as we call it, than that. And I wrote it.
text_no:
Jeg likte henne, og jeg gikk ut med henne. Jeg husker at jeg måtte enten gifte meg med henne, spørre om hun ville gifte seg med meg, eller la henne gå. Hun var for god til å bare bruke tiden hennes slik. Hun ville bli en god kone for noen. Jeg ønsket ikke å ødelegge livet hennes.
Jeg sa til meg selv, "Jeg må ta en avgjørelse nå, men jeg har ikke mot til å spørre henne." Jeg lurte på hva jeg skulle gjøre.
Kanskje du lurer på hvordan jeg til slutt spurte henne. Jeg prøvde å spørre henne flere ganger, men du vet hvordan det oppstår en stor klump i halsen, og du ikke kan svelge når du prøver å si noe? Hver gang jeg planla å spørre, sa jeg til meg selv, "I kveld skal jeg gjøre det. Ja, jeg gjør det." Jeg sa til henne, "Om ti minutter, etter klokken min, skal jeg spørre." Men hver gang hun rullet med øynene, klarte jeg det ikke. Jeg kunne ikke få spørsmålet ut.
Så du lurer sikkert på hvordan vi egentlig giftet oss. Jeg skrev et brev til henne og spurte henne. Ja, jeg skrev et brev. Det var ikke bare et "Kjære frøken..." Det hadde litt mer, som vi kaller det, søt snakk enn som så. Og jeg skrev det.

text:
And I remember I wrote it all out, and I asked her if she'd marry me. And I didn't have nerve enough to give it to her, so I just put it in the mail. So I put it on Monday morning, went on to work.
I had a date with her for Wednesday night to go to church. And so I… As Wednesday night begin to come along, I begin to think about it, "What if her mother got a hold of that letter, and that … and she didn't get it?"
And then her dad and I were very good friends. Her mother too, but her dad was just a fine old Dutchman. And he… But her mother, she was a--she was kind of a little fritzie, you know, and she… I guess she thought I was a little trashy for her daughter. And so I… She was a good woman, but I was--just wasn't up in the place to marry her. That was all I know. And she didn't think so much of me. But I tried to treat her nice, but I just couldn't get on the good side of her somehow.
text_no:
Jeg husker at jeg skrev det hele ut og spurte henne om hun ville gifte seg med meg. Jeg hadde ikke mot til å gi det til henne personlig, så jeg sendte det i posten på mandag morgen før jeg dro på jobb.
Jeg hadde en avtale med henne om å gå til menigheten på onsdag kveld. Etter hvert som onsdag nærmet seg, begynte jeg å bekymre meg: "Hva om moren hennes fikk tak i brevet, og hun ikke mottok det?"
Hennes far og jeg var svært gode venner. Moren hennes også, men faren var en god, gammel nederlender. Moren, derimot, var litt mer vanvittig, og jeg tror hun mente jeg ikke var god nok for hennes datter. Hun var en god kvinne, men jeg var ikke på et sted i livet hvor jeg var klar for å gifte meg med henne. Det er alt jeg vet. Hun hadde ikke høye tanker om meg. Jeg prøvde å behandle henne pent, men klarte aldri helt å komme på godfot med henne.

text:
So I remember, I got to thinking about it, and I was scared to death to go up there that night. So I got my old Ford finally, dressed up with the best that I owned, you know, and went up there and stopped in front of the house. And I knowed better than to blow the horn. Oh, my. She was a lady. Yes, sir.
If your girl… If you love her enough to go with her, go in like a man and enough to get her. That's right.
text_no:
Jeg husker at jeg tenkte på det, og jeg var livredd for å dra dit den kvelden. Så jeg tok den gamle Forden min, kledde meg i det beste jeg hadde, og dro dit. Jeg stoppet foran huset, og jeg visste bedre enn å tute med hornet. Å nei, hun var en dame, ja, sir.
Hvis du elsker jenta di nok til å være sammen med henne, så gå inn som en mann og ha mot nok til å hente henne. Det er riktig.

text:
So I'm… I knew better than to blow my horn. So I got out, walked out of the car, and walked up to the door. And I thought, "Oh, my, this is all of it." I [Brother Branham knocks.] knocked at the door like that. And oh, my heart was racing as hard as it could, you know. And I thought, "Who's going to come to the door." I could just see her mother come look at me, "William, I got that letter." Oh, my.
So I said… Hope come to the door, and she said, "Oh, hello, Billy."
And I said, "Hi, Hope." Her name was Hope. And I said…
She said, "Come in."
I thought, "Oh, oh. Uh-huh, they're getting me inside. And then I know I haven't got a running chance then. What--what will I do about that?" So I said, "Well, I--I--I will just wait out here. It's awfully warm."
And she said, "Oh, step in. Mother wants to see you."
And I thought, "Oh, no."
You know how Satan can lie to you, you know, and tell you, that's it. That's it. So don't never… Circumstantial evidence won't do every time (You see?), so…
text_no:
Jeg visste bedre enn å tute med hornet. Jeg gikk ut av bilen og gikk opp til døren. Jeg tenkte, "Å, nei, nå skjer det." Jeg banket på døren. Hjertet mitt hamret som aldri før. Jeg lurte på hvem som kom til å åpne døren. Jeg kunne bare se for meg moren hennes kikke på meg og si: "William, jeg fikk det brevet." Å, hjelp.
Hope kom til døren og sa: "Hei, Billy."
Jeg svarte: "Hei, Hope." Hun het Hope.
Hun sa: "Kom inn."
Jeg tenkte, "Å nei, nå får de meg inn. Da har jeg ingen sjanse til å stikke av. Hva skal jeg gjøre med det?" Så jeg sa, "Vel, jeg venter bare her ute. Det er veldig varmt."
Hun sa: "Å, kom inn. Mor vil se deg."
Jeg tenkte, "Å nei."
Du vet hvordan Satan kan lyve for deg og si at nå er det slutt. Men husk, omstendighetsbeviser holder ikke alltid.

text:
I stepped in the door with my hat off, with all my Sunday manners. My, I was just best that I knowed how to act. And I said, "Sure is warm, isn't it? My." Just going.
She said, "Yeah, I'll be ready just in a few minutes."
And so her mother come in, and she spoke just as nice. And I thought, "Oh, oh. She never got that letter. Uh-huh."
So then I got feeling pretty good. And so, I went on down; we went to church, and she said, "Let's just walk to church tonight instead of going in the car."
I thought, "Oh, oh. She got it." Ha [unclear words].
So we went down and walked to church. I never heard a word Doctor Davis said that night. He preached and preached, and I was setting there thinking, "Yep, this is my last date. She'll tell me as soon we get out here, 'That's all off now. I got your letter, and that's…'" You know how [unclear words] think, you know. You keep thinking, it'll be a realizing to you after while, you know.
text_no:
Jeg gikk inn døren med hatten av og viste alle mine beste manerer. Jeg oppførte meg så korrekt som jeg bare kunne. Jeg sa: "Det er sikkert varmt, ikke sant?" 
Hun svarte: "Ja, jeg er klar om noen få minutter."
Hennes mor kom inn og hilste like vennlig som alltid. Jeg tenkte: "Å nei, hun fikk aldri det brevet."
Jeg begynte å føle meg litt bedre. Vi gikk til menigheten, og hun sa: "La oss gå til menigheten i kveld i stedet for å ta bilen."
Jeg tenkte: "Å nei, hun fikk det." Ha.
Vi gikk til menigheten. Jeg hørte ikke et ord Doktor Davis sa den kvelden. Han talte og talte, men jeg satt der og tenkte: "Dette er min siste date. Hun kommer til å fortelle meg etter møtet at det er slutt fordi hun har mottatt brevet mitt." Du vet hvordan tankene kan løpe løpsk, og etter hvert vil du innse hva som skjer.

text:
I could just hear her saying this was all, and I thought. "Oh, isn't she pretty. Isn't she a nice lady. Don't I hate to hear this time come." I didn't even hear what the preacher was saying.
After the service was over, we started walking on down home. She never said nothing. I go along. When we come out from under the trees, you know, the moon shining bright. And I'd look over in those dark eyes, you know. And I said, "I hate to hear her say it, but I--I…"
After while I got pretty brave. I thought, "She never got the letter. It just stuck in the box; that's all." Got to breathing better. I said, "She'd done named it to me before now, if she'd have got that letter." So I was going along, you know, feeling pretty good then. And I was walking right along.
We was walking along, she said, "Billy."
And I said, "Yes."
She said, "I got your letter." Oh… Oh, my. And then she just walked on, never said a thing.
I said, "You did?"
She said, "Uh-huh." That's all. Just went walking… You know how a woman can do, just keep you in suspense, you know. She said… Just walked on, never said a thing.
And I said, "Uh… Hmmph… Well, uh… Well, uh, did you read it?"
She said, "Uh-huh." Oh, she'd got it.
I said, "Did you read all of it?"
She said, "Uh-huh." That's all she said, just kept on.
And I thought, "Oh, girl, do something and [unclear words] kill me." And just on like that. And she just kept on going like that. And after while, oh, I said, "What'd you think about it?"
She said, "It was all right."
Well, we got married [unclear words]. We got married; that was it. So…
text_no:
Jeg kunne nesten høre henne si at dette var alt, og jeg tenkte: "Å, er hun ikke vakker. Er hun ikke en hyggelig dame. Jeg gruer meg til denne tiden kommer." Jeg hørte knapt hva forkynneren sa.
Etter at møtet var over, begynte vi å gå hjemover. Hun sa ingenting. Vi gikk videre. Da vi kom ut fra trærne, vet du, månen skinte klart. Jeg så inn i de mørke øynene hennes. Jeg sa: "Jeg gruer meg til å høre henne si det, men jeg..."
Etter en stund ble jeg ganske modig. Jeg tenkte: "Hun fikk aldri brevet. Det ble bare liggende i postkassen; det er alt." Jeg begynte å puste lettere. Jeg sa til meg selv: "Hun ville ha nevnt det nå, hvis hun hadde fått brevet." Så jeg følte meg ganske bra, og vi gikk videre.
Mens vi gikk, sa hun: "Billy."
Jeg svarte: "Ja."
Hun sa: "Jeg fikk brevet ditt." Åh... Åh, nei. Så gikk hun bare videre uten å si noe mer.
Jeg sa: "Gjorde du det?"
Hun svarte: "Mm-hmm." Det var alt. Hun bare gikk videre. Du vet hvordan en kvinne kan holde deg i spenning. Hun sa ingenting mer.
Jeg sa: "Øh... Hmmph... Vel, øh... Vel, øh, leste du det?"
Hun svarte: "Mm-hmm." Åh, hun hadde fått det.
Jeg sa: "Leste du alt?"
Hun svarte: "Mm-hmm." Det var alt hun sa, og fortsatte å gå.
Og jeg tenkte: "Å, jente, gjør noe, si noe. Skal dette drepe meg?" Og hun bare fortsatte på samme måte. Etter en stund, sa jeg: "Hva syntes du om det?"
Hun svarte: "Det var greit."
Vel, vi giftet oss. Vi giftet oss; det var det. Så...

text:
But one more thing. When she was… I remember she told me I had to ask her mother for her. Oh, my. I said, "Honey, look. Let's you and I make an agreement. See, we're supposed to be fifty-fifty on these things. You ask your mother, and I'll ask your daddy."
She said, "All right. Very well."
I said, "That's all right."
And so, I thought I could get by pretty well with Charlie, because I'd… He liked me real well. And I… He understood me more.
text_no:
Én ting til. Jeg husker at hun fortalte meg at jeg måtte spørre moren hennes om å få henne. Å, kjære. Jeg sa: "Kjære, la oss inngå en avtale. Vi skal være femti-femti i slike saker. Du spør moren din, og jeg spør faren din."
Hun svarte: "Greit. Veldig godt."
Jeg sa: "Det er bra."
Jeg trodde jeg kunne komme godt overens med Charlie, for han likte meg godt og forstod meg bedre.

text:
So that night, I remember I had to ask. I sat there and I… My, I wasn't having me a good time at all. He was playing the Victrola, you know. And I went outside. I got to the door, and she looked at me. You know, I was going to go without asking him, you know. And I said… And Charlie was setting there typing on the typewriter, you know, and it was nine-thirty. Time I had to go [unclear word] He said…
I walked to the door, and I said, "Hmmph, Charlie?"
He said, "Yes, Bill."
I said, "Uh--uh… Could I speak to you out here just a minute?"
He said, "Yes?"
He looked over at Mrs. Brumbach, and she looked at me, you know. Oh, oh, oh, oh. And I said, "Here's where it all ends is right here."
We went outside. Then I thought maybe that Hope had already told her mother, and her mother had done told him to say "No," you know. So I had it all fixed out how it was going to be.
"Well, how are you, Bill?"
I said, "Oh, pretty good." I said, "Sure is a nice night tonight, isn't it, Charlie."
He said, "It sure is, Bill." He said, "Yes, Bill. You can have her." I started… Oh, my, what… I like him yet today. He just went home to glory a few weeks ago. God bless his soul. You don't know what he saved me then.
text_no:
Den kvelden husker jeg at jeg måtte spørre. Jeg satt der, og jeg… Du verden, jeg hadde det virkelig ikke bra. Han spilte Victrola, vet du. Jeg gikk ut. Jeg kom til døren, og hun så på meg. Jeg skulle gå uten å spørre ham, vet du. Og jeg sa... Og Charlie satt der og skrev på skrivemaskinen, vet du, og klokken var halv ti. Tiden var inne. Han sa…
Jeg gikk til døren og sa, "Hmmph, Charlie?"
Han sa, "Ja, Bill."
Jeg sa, "Øh... Kan jeg snakke med deg ute et øyeblikk?"
Han sa, "Ja?"
Han så bort på fru Brumbach, og hun så på meg, vet du. Åh, åh, åh, åh. Og jeg tenkte, "Her er det det hele slutter."
Vi gikk ut. Jeg trodde kanskje at Hope allerede hadde fortalt moren sin, og at moren hennes hadde sagt til ham å si "Nei," vet du. Så jeg hadde alt planlagt hvordan det skulle bli.
"Vel, hvordan har du det, Bill?"
Jeg sa, "Åh, ganske bra." Jeg sa, "Det er en fin kveld i kveld, ikke sant, Charlie?"
Han sa, "Det er det, Bill." Han sa, "Ja, Bill. Du kan få henne." Jeg begynte... Å, herlighet, hva... Jeg liker ham fortsatt i dag. Han gikk hjem til herligheten for noen uker siden. Gud velsigne hans sjel. Du aner ikke hva han reddet meg fra da.

text:
I said, "Charlie, look. I'm as poor as I can be. I'm working down here in a ditch for twenty cents an hour." But I said, "I love her with all my heart. I can't clothe her, and feed her, dress her the way you can. But, Charlie, I'll say this: I'll be as good to her as I know how to be. I'll work until my hands run blood to make her a living."
He put his hand over on me, he said, "Billy, look. I'd rather you to have her and be good to her. After all, happiness does not consist of how much of the world's goods you own, but how contented you are with the portions that's allotted to you." That's right.
I said, "Well, Charlie, I'll be just as good to her as I know how to be."
text_no:
Jeg sa, "Charlie, se her. Jeg er så fattig som jeg kan være. Jeg jobber her nede i en grøft for tjue cent i timen." Men jeg sa, "Jeg elsker henne av hele mitt hjerte. Jeg kan ikke kle henne, mate henne eller pynte henne slik du kan. Men, Charlie, jeg vil si dette: Jeg skal være så god mot henne som jeg bare kan. Jeg skal jobbe til hendene mine blør for å gi henne et livsgrunnlag."
Han la hånden sin på meg og sa, "Billy, hør her. Jeg vil heller at du skal ha henne og være god mot henne. Tross alt, lykke handler ikke om hvor mye av verdens eiendeler du har, men hvor fornøyd du er med det som er tildelt deg." Det stemmer.
Jeg sa, "Vel, Charlie, jeg skal være så god mot henne som jeg bare kan."

text:
And we was married. And when we got married, we didn't have nothing to go to housekeeping with. We was very poor. I didn't… I was the one married her, and she was the one taken me to … 'for I to support. And we were happy, just as happy, some of the happiest days of my life.
I'd just… Along during that time, I'd just been ordained to be a minister. I didn't have no church as yet but we was just preaching around wherever I could in tent meetings and so forth. And I went to work.
And I never will forget how we set up housekeeping. We went and rented two rooms for four dollars a month. Who doesn't know, that wasn't much. And some lady give us an old folding bed. Did you ever see one, the folding beds? And I went down to Sears and Roebuck and got me one of these little breakfast sets without being painted. And I remember, I painted them. And right… On the seat and on the table, I painted a big shamrock, being Irish, you know. And so I painted a big shamrock. And we went to housekeeping. I went over to Mr. Weber's, Brother Curtis back there, one of his--some of his people, and he dealt in used goods. And I bought an old secondhanded cooking stove for a dollar and seventy-five cents. And I paid, I believe it's a dollar for new grates and put in it. And we started to housekeeping.
text_no:
Vi giftet oss. Da vi giftet oss, hadde vi ingenting å starte husholdning med. Vi var svært fattige. Jeg giftet meg med henne, og hun tok meg til mann, for at jeg skulle forsørge oss. Vi var lykkelige, noen av de lykkeligste dagene i mitt liv.
På den tiden hadde jeg nettopp blitt ordinert som forkynner. Jeg hadde ikke noen menighet ennå, men jeg forkynte rundt omkring, på teltmøter og lignende. Jeg begynte å arbeide.
Jeg vil aldri glemme hvordan vi startet husholdningen. Vi leide to rom for fire dollar i måneden. Det var ikke mye. En dame ga oss en gammel sammenleggbar seng. Har du noen gang sett en slik, en sammenleggbar seng? Jeg dro ned til Sears og Roebuck og kjøpte et av disse små frokostsettene som ikke var malt. Jeg husker at jeg malte dem. På setet og på bordet malte jeg en stor kløver, siden jeg er irsk, vet du. Slik startet vi husholdningen. Jeg gikk over til Mr. Weber's, Bror Curtis der borte, en av hans slektninger, og han drev med brukte varer. Jeg kjøpte en gammel brukt komfyr for en dollar og syttifem cent. Jeg betalte, tror jeg, en dollar for nye rister og satte dem i. Slik startet vi husholdningen.

text:
But we were happy. We were happy as we could be. We just had one another, and that's all we cared for. We loved the Lord with all of our hearts. And that's how we lived, just as happy as we could be.
And I remember one day then I wanted to go on a little fishing trip up at Mishawaka, Indiana. That was my first time to ever come in contact with any Pentecostal people. And I went up to old Brother Ryan's and went fishing. On my road back, they was having a… It was the P. A. of W., I believe it is, or P. A. of J. C. I think the organization's died out and gone now, but--or reunited with some other organization.
text_no:
Men vi var glade. Så glade som vi kunne være. Vi hadde hverandre, og det var alt vi brydde oss om. Vi elsket Herren av hele vårt hjerte. Og slik levde vi, så lykkelige som vi kunne være.
Jeg husker en dag da jeg ville dra på en liten fisketur til Mishawaka, Indiana. Det var første gang jeg kom i kontakt med pinsefolk. Jeg dro til gamle Bror Ryan og fisket. På vei tilbake oppdaget jeg at de holdt et møte. Det var P. A. of W. eller kanskje P. A. of J. C. Jeg tror organisasjonen enten har gått ut eller slått seg sammen med en annen, men uansett...

text:
But anyhow, there's a preacher by the name of Rowe at Mishawaka, had the tabernacle. Some of you might know him, a Reverend Rowe. Well, yes, there's people with their hands up knows Reverend Rowe. Well, it was at his tabernacle.
I was coming back, and I seen such a crowd of people and heard such a noise, and I thought, "Well, where in the world's all that noise coming from." And I went down there. It was religious people. And they was a screaming, and shouting, and jumping, and running, and carrying on. I thought, "What kind of a bunch of people is that?"
So I drove my old Ford over to one side. I only had about a dollar and a quarter, and to live on. And so… Enough gasoline to get back home, about two hundred and fifty miles.
And I walked over there and went in. And those people, I never seen such church manners in my life. Umm, my. They were dancing; they were running; they were screaming.
text_no:
Det var en forkynner ved navn Rowe i Mishawaka som hadde et tabernakel. Noen av dere kjenner kanskje til ham, Pastor Rowe. Ja, jeg ser flere som rekker opp hånden og kjenner Pastor Rowe. Det var i hans tabernakel.
Jeg var på vei tilbake og så en stor folkemengde og hørte mye støy. Jeg tenkte, "Hvor i all verden kommer all den lyden fra?" Jeg gikk ned dit og oppdaget at det var religiøse mennesker. De skrek, ropte, hoppet, løp og oppførte seg veldig livlig. Jeg tenkte, "Hva slags gruppe mennesker er dette?"
Jeg parkerte min gamle Ford på siden. Jeg hadde bare rundt en dollar og tjuefem cent å leve på og nok bensin til å komme meg hjem igjen, omtrent 250 miles unna.
Jeg gikk bort dit og gikk inn. Aldri hadde jeg sett slike menighetsmanerer. De danset, løp og skrek høyt.

text:
Why, I said, "What kind of a people is this?" I thought, "I'll just slip inside the door and watch what they're doing." Why, they're clapping their hands, and they're screaming, and some of them beating the tambourine, and some running up and down the boards, and some dancing and running around." I thought, "Well, what's wrong with those people?" Never seen anything like that. So I got inside the door.
Now, it never rubbed off, but it begin to get on me. I begin to looking around; I thought, "Well, you know what? They're awful happy, awful free. They're just a little bit more freer than I am." So I said, "Maybe the Lord's got something that I don't know nothing about." So I begin to look at them.
And somehow, I begin to get a love. I seen they loved one another. And those women would grab one another, hug one another, and kiss each other; and the men throwed their arms around one another, and hugged each other. Why, I never seen that before.
I said, "Say, this is--looks good to me. Believe I'll just stay. They said we're going to have services tonight."
text_no:
Jeg sa: "Hva slags folk er dette?" Jeg tenkte: "Jeg går bare inn døra og ser hva de gjør." De klapper i hendene, de roper, noen slår på tamburin, noen springer frem og tilbake, og noen danser. Jeg tenkte: "Hva er det galt med disse menneskene?" Jeg hadde aldri sett noe lignende. Så jeg gikk inn døra.
Nå, det smittet aldri over på meg, men det begynte å påvirke meg. Jeg begynte å se meg rundt og tenkte: "De er veldig glade og veldig frie. De er litt friere enn meg." Så jeg sa: "Kanskje Herren har noe som jeg ikke vet om." Så jeg begynte å følge med på dem.
Og på en eller annen måte begynte jeg å føle en kjærlighet. Jeg så at de elsket hverandre. Kvinnene klemte hverandre og kysset hverandre; og mennene la armene rundt hverandre og klemte hverandre. Jeg hadde aldri sett det før.
Jeg sa: "Dette ser bra ut for meg. Jeg tror jeg blir. De sa de skulle ha møter i kveld."

text:
So I had a dollar seventy-five cents. And I said, "No. I've got to spend at least one more dollar of that to go home on. Now, it'll leave me seventy-five cents. I can't rent a room." So I went down and got me about two dozen rolls. And I said, "I can live off these for a few days. I'm going to look around here and see what this is all about." So I went out in the--got me a place in a cornfield located, where I could sleep that night.
I come back down to the service. And that night, he said, "I want all the preachers to come to the platform." And I guess there's three or four hundred preachers got on the platform.
They was having a conference. And they had to have it up there on account of the… Well, the southern states wouldn't let the colored and white together. So they was having it up there. And I noticed all them preachers.
text_no:
Jeg hadde en dollar og syttifem cent. Jeg tenkte: "Nei, jeg må spare minst én dollar til hjemreisen. Det gir meg syttifem cent igjen. Det holder ikke til å leie et rom." Så jeg kjøpte rundt to dusin rundstykker. Jeg tenkte: "Disse kan jeg leve på i noen dager mens jeg undersøker hva som foregår her." Jeg fant et sted i en maisåker hvor jeg kunne sove den natten.
Senere gikk jeg tilbake til møtet. Den kvelden sa han: "Jeg vil at alle forkynnerne skal komme opp på plattformen." Jeg antar at tre eller fire hundre forkynnere stilte seg opp på plattformen.
De hadde en konferanse, og den måtte holdes der på grunn av forholdene. De sørlige statene tillot ikke at fargede og hvite var sammen. Så de hadde konferansen der. Jeg la merke til alle forkynnerne.

text:
And that night, they had their main speaker was an old colored man. They had to lead him out to the platform. Had on one of them little bitty--them cut away preacher coats, you know, the belt and collar. Just a little rim of white hair, and the poor old fellow come out there.
And all that ministers that day had been speaking about Christ, and how great He was and everything. I was listening to them.
Said, "All the preachers come to the platform." I went up and set down with them. "We only have time," they said, "just to have the preachers say who he is and where he's from."
I just raised up, and I said, "Billy Branham, Jeffersonville," set down. The rest of them along like that. Went on along down the line.
text_no:
Den kvelden hadde de en eldre farget mann som hovedtaler. De måtte lede ham ut til plattformen. Han hadde på seg en av disse små, tradisjonelle forkynnerfrakkene med belte og krage. Han hadde en smal stripe av hvitt hår, og den stakkars gamle mannen kom frem.
Alle forkynnerne den dagen hadde snakket om Kristus, hvor stor Han var og alt det andre. Jeg lyttet til dem.
De sa: "Alle forkynnere kom fram på plattformen." Jeg gikk opp og satte meg med dem. "Vi har bare tid," sa de, "til at forkynnerne kan si hvem de er og hvor de kommer fra."
Jeg reiste meg kort opp og sa: "Billy Branham, Jeffersonville," og satte meg igjen. De andre gjorde det samme etter tur.

text:
This old preacher come out to preach. He said he had--was going to preach the message that night. And the old fellow come out. And I thought, "Poor old brother. He was just all crippled up like this."
He come out. And he took his text from over in, I believe, in Job 7:27, or somewhere there. "Where were you when I laid the foundations of the world?" Said he, "When the morning stars sang together, and the sons of God shouted for joy." And instead of preaching what He'd been done out here on earth, he took Him up back yonder about ten thousand years before the world was ever formed, brought Him on down across through the skies, and come down the horizontal rainbow back yonder in eternity somewhere.
When that old fellow got anointed, he jumped up in the air, kicked his heels together, hollered, "Whoopie." Walked off of that platform, looked around. He had more room than I've got up here. He said, "You ain't got room enough up here for me to preach." Walked off.
I said, "That's what I want. If it'll make an old man act like that, what would it do to me?" I said, "That's the thing I want. That's what I want." I said, "My, what a wonderful people."
text_no:
Denne gamle forkynneren kom ut for å tale. Han sa at han skulle forkynne budskapet den kvelden. Den gamle mannen kom ut. Jeg tenkte, «Stakkars, gamle bror. Han var helt krøplig.»
Han kom ut og tok sin tekst fra Job 7:27, tror jeg. «Hvor var du da Jeg la jordens grunnvoll?» Sa han, «Da morgenstjernene jublet sammen, og Guds sønner ropte av glede.» I stedet for å tale om det som hadde skjedd her på jorden, tok han oss med tilbake ti tusen år før verden ble dannet. Han førte oss gjennom himmelen og nedover den horisontale regnbuen inn i evigheten et sted.
Når den gamle mannen ble salvet, hoppet han opp i luften, klasket hælene sammen og ropte, «Whoopie!» Han gikk av plattformen, så seg rundt og sa, «Dere har ikke nok plass her oppe til at jeg kan preke.» Så gikk han av.
Jeg sa, «Det er det jeg vil ha. Hvis det kan få en gammel mann til å oppføre seg slik, hva ville det da gjøre med meg?» Jeg sa, «Det er det jeg vil ha. Det er det jeg vil ha.» Jeg tenkte, «For en fantastisk menneskegruppe.»

text:
I went out that night, and I got down in the old cornfield, and I prayed and I prayed. Nobody knowed me. So I took my trousers and put them between the seats of the Ford and pressed them, you know. Took the back seat out and the front seat out, put them down. Probably you've done the same thing, pressed them overnight. I laid down over there in the grass, and I prayed nearly all night.
And next morning they said they was going to have breakfast at ten o'clock. I wouldn't eat with them, because I had no money to put in the offering. And I just had my rolls. So I eat my rolls, and come by a hydrant down there, and got me some water, come on down. Now, I'd been welcome, but I just didn't want to do it, because I couldn't help them out. So I didn't have the money to… But I was wondering what they had spiritually. And I…
And then that morning, they started singing that little song, "I Know It Was The Blood, I Know It Was The Blood." And oh, my, they was having a real time.
text_no:
Den kvelden gikk jeg ut og ba i maisåkeren. Ingen visste hvem jeg var. Jeg tok buksene mine, la dem mellom setene i Forden og presset dem. Jeg tok ut baksetet og forsetet og la dem ned. Kanskje du har gjort det samme, presset klærne over natten. Jeg la meg ned i gresset og ba nesten hele natten.
Neste morgen skulle de ha frokost klokken ti. Jeg ville ikke spise med dem, fordi jeg ikke hadde penger til å gi i kollekten, og jeg hadde bare mine rundstykker. Jeg spiste rundstykkene mine, stoppet ved en vannkran og fikk litt vann, og fortsatte min vei. Jeg ville vært velkommen, men jeg ønsket ikke å være til bry siden jeg ikke kunne bidra økonomisk. Jeg lurte på hva de hadde å tilby åndelig.
Senere den morgenen begynte de å synge sangen "Jeg Vet Det Var Blodet." Å, du store, de hadde det virkelig fint.

text:
So after they got through with all the jubilee part, then he said, "Last night on the platform, there was a young minister by the name of Billy Branham."
I thought, "Oh, oh."
Said, "If he is in the building, tell him to come forth and speak for us this morning." Well, I never even seen a microphone before. And I was sitting back there with a pair of seersucker trousers on and a little T-shirt. I just hunkered down real easy, you know.
So they said… That fellow come up again, Mr. Kurt, you all may know of him. Yeah [unclear words] from Cincinnati. Reverend Kurt, he's a chart teacher he was there at the meeting. He said, "Anybody outside know where Reverend William Branham from Jeffersonville." Said, "Tell him to come up at the platform and come to the service."
I got down real low like this. I was sitting right beside of a colored man. He looked over to me; he said, "Do you know that guy?"
My, what was I going to do? I just couldn't lie. I said, "Yes, sir."
And he said, "Well, go find him."
Well, what was I going to do? I just couldn't lie to the man. I said, "Hold over here a minute, brother, and I'll tell you something [unclear word]." I said, "I'm he. But I can't…"
"You is?"
I said, "Yes." I said, "I can't…"
Said, "Go on up there."
I said, "Let me [unclear words]." I said, "I--I got on these seersucker trousers and this T-shirt." I said, "I can't go up."
Said, "Them people don't care what you dress like. Get on up there."
I said, "No, no."
And in just in a few minutes, he said, "Did anybody find Reverend Branham?"
That colored man said, "Here he is. Here he is. Here he is."
Seersucker trousers, T-shirt, talk about [unclear words]. Wonder what [unclear words]. My [unclear words] you know.
All them people looked at me, them people who really have their religion, you know. And me up there my old cold Baptist ways, you know, and the … got up there, you know.
And I said, "My." I thought, "Lord, if You ever helped anybody, You help me." I said, "I'm grateful to…" I finally thought, "Well, what am I going to read? Or what am I going to do?" I was so nervous, I just couldn't hardly hold myself together.
text_no:
Etter at de var ferdige med jubeldelen, sa han: "I går kveld på plattformen var det en ung forkynner ved navn Billy Branham."
Jeg tenkte: "Å nei."
Han sa: "Hvis han er i bygningen, be ham komme frem og tale for oss i morges." Vel, jeg hadde aldri sett en mikrofon før. Jeg satt der i et par seersucker-bukser og en liten T-skjorte. Jeg prøvde å krype sammen så usynlig som mulig.
Så sa de... Den fyren kom opp igjen, Mr. Kurt, dere kjenner kanskje til ham. Ja, [uklare ord] fra Cincinnati. Pastor Kurt var en lærer som underviste i tabellforklaringer, og han var til stede på møtet. Han sa: "Er det noen her ute som vet hvor Pastor William Branham fra Jeffersonville er? Be ham komme opp på plattformen og delta i møtet."
Jeg krympet meg sammen så lavt jeg kunne. Jeg satt rett ved siden av en farget mann. Han så bort på meg og spurte: "Kjenner du den fyren?"
Herlighet, hva skulle jeg gjøre? Jeg kunne ikke lyve. Jeg svarte: "Ja, sir."
Han sa: "Vel, gå og finn ham."
Hva skulle jeg gjøre? Jeg kunne ikke lyve. Jeg sa: "Hold an et øyeblikk, bror, så skal jeg fortelle deg noe." Jeg sa: "Det er meg. Men jeg kan ikke..."
"Er det deg?"
Jeg sa: "Ja." Jeg sa: "Men jeg kan ikke..."
Han sa: "Gå opp dit."
Jeg sa: "La meg forklare." Jeg sa: "Jeg har på meg disse seersucker-buksene og denne T-skjorten." Jeg sa: "Jeg kan ikke gå opp."
Han sa: "De folkene bryr seg ikke om hva du har på deg. Kom deg opp dit."
Jeg sa: "Nei, nei."
Og etter noen få minutter sa de: "Fant noen Pastor Branham?"
Den fargede mannen ropte: "Her er han. Her er han. Her er han."
Seersucker-bukser, T-skjorte, snakk om [uklare ord]. Lurer på hva [uklare ord]. Herlighet [uklare ord] du vet.
Alle de folkene så på meg, de folkene som virkelig har sin religion, vet du. Og jeg der oppe med mine gamle kalde baptistmåter, du vet, og... kom opp dit, du vet.
Og jeg sa: "Herlighet." Jeg tenkte: "Herre, hvis Du noen gang har hjulpet noen, hjelp meg nå." Jeg sa: "Jeg er takknemlig for..." Jeg tenkte til slutt: "Vel, hva skal jeg lese? Eller hva skal jeg gjøre?" Jeg var så nervøs at jeg nesten ikke klarte å holde meg sammen.

text:
And I got over here, and I jumped over to Luke there where it said, "The rich man lifted up his eyes in hell and then he cried." I just happened to fall on, "And then he cried." I took that little three words, "And then he cried." I started talking. Everybody got to hollering, "Amen." And then I cried.
First thing you know, about two hours later, become… [Blank spot on tape.]
Outside. The next thing I know, It done got on me or something. I got out of that [unclear words]. [Blank spot on tape.]
Some fellow walked up with a big pair of boots on, big Texas hat, said, "I'm Reverend So-and-so." Elder, I believe he called his name.
I said, "Are you a preacher?" Them Texas boots on, and a big hat. Well, I'm not so bad off after all. I said, "You a preacher?"
"Yes, sir. I'm a Pentecostal preacher. I…" Said, "Why don't you come down to Texas and hold a revival for me."
I said, "Me?" He said… I said, "Look--look, brother," I said, "I--I just don't know religion that good."
He said, "I don't care. Come on down, I like it," he told me.
text_no:
Da jeg kom hit, gikk jeg til Lukas der det sto: "Den rike mannen løftet sine øyne i helvete og da ropte han." Jeg kom tilfeldigvis over ordene "Og da ropte han." Jeg tok de tre ordene, "Og da ropte han," og begynte å tale. Alle begynte å rope "Amen." Og så ropte jeg.
Før jeg visste ordet av det, hadde det gått omtrent to timer. [Tomt område på lydbånd.]
Utenfor. Det neste jeg visste, hadde noe kommet over meg. Jeg kom meg ut av [uklare ord]. [Tomt område på lydbånd.]
En kar med store støvler og en stor Texas-hatt kom bort og sa: "Jeg er Pastor Så-og-så." Eldste, tror jeg han sa at han het.
Jeg spurte: "Er du forkynner?" Med de Texas-støvlene og den store hatten. Vel, jeg er kanskje ikke så ille stilt tross alt. Jeg spurte: "Er du forkynner?"
"Ja, sir. Jeg er en pinsevenn-forkynner. Hvorfor kommer du ikke til Texas og holder en vekkelse for meg?"
Jeg sa: "Meg?" Han svarte… Jeg sa: "Se her, Bror, jeg kjenner ikke religion så godt."
Han svarte: "Det bryr jeg meg ikke om. Kom ned, jeg liker det," sa han.

text:
About that time a fellow tapped me on the shoulder, said he was a preacher. And he had on a little old knicker-bocker golf pants like, you know. He was a preacher from Florida.
I said, "Well, my seersucker trousers are not so bad after all."
Well, I looked around like that, and they had [unclear words]. A woman come up was a missionary to the Indians. And why, I had all kind of places to… Why, my, you don't know the places I had to go.
And I went out there and got down in the cornfield, and I just praised the Lord for giving me the opportunity, jumped in my old Ford, making forty miles an hour: Twenty miles this way, and twenty miles up-and-down this a way. Down the road [unclear words].
text_no:
Omtrent på den tiden ble jeg tappet på skulderen av en mann som sa han var forkynner. Han hadde på seg et par gamle golfbukser. Han var en forkynner fra Florida.
Jeg sa: "Vel, mine seersucker-bukser er ikke så verst likevel."
Jeg kikket rundt, og der var det flere folk. En kvinne kom bort; hun var misjonær til indianerne. Du aner ikke hvor mange steder jeg måtte dra til.
Jeg gikk ut i maisåkeren, og jeg bare priste Herren for at Han ga meg muligheten. Jeg hoppet inn i min gamle Ford, som kjørte i førti mil i timen: tjue mil denne veien og tjue mil opp og ned den andre veien. Nedover veien fortsatte jeg.

text:
When I come home… When I got home, my wife, bless her heart, she was waiting as usual. She'd run out and meet me. She seen me coming. She's real … had a little old long black hair, pretty eye--brown eyes. She come running to me. She threw her arms around me, her and the baby. And she said, "Oh, I just know you've had a good time fishing and … up there in the lake."
I said, "Honey, I want to tell you what I done." I said, "I--I met the best people in the world."
She said, "Well, what?"
I said, "The best people in the world. You talk about people that's not ashamed of their religion, you ought to see them." I said, "They clap their hands, and they scream, they run all over the floor, and everything."
She said, "What?"
And I said, "Yes."
She said, "Where they at."
I said, "Up at Mishawaka." I said, "I'm going to tell you something. Looky here." I pulled out a long piece of paper. I said, "They want me to hold revivals for them all over the country."
She said, "You?"
And I said, "Yes."
She said, "Sure enough. Is that right?"
And I said, "Yes, sir. They told me I could hold revivals for them."
And she said, "Well…"
I said, "Will you go with me?"
She said, "Sure." Bless her heart. She said, "Sure, I'll go." And that's a real wife, go with you through thick or thin.
text_no:
Da jeg kom hjem... Min kone, Gud velsigne henne, ventet som vanlig. Hun løp ut og møtte meg. Hun så meg komme. Hun hadde langt svart hår og vakre brune øyne. Hun kom løpende mot meg, kastet armene rundt meg, sammen med babyen. Hun sa: "Åh, jeg bare vet at du har hatt en fin tid med å fiske der oppe ved innsjøen."
Jeg svarte: "Kjære, jeg vil fortelle deg hva jeg har gjort. Jeg møtte de beste menneskene i verden."
Hun sa: "Hva?"
Jeg sa: "De beste menneskene i verden. Du snakker om folk som ikke er skamfulle over sin tro, du burde se dem. De klapper i hendene, skriker, løper rundt på gulvet og alt mulig."
Hun sa: "Hva?"
Og jeg sa: "Ja."
Hun spurte: "Hvor er de?"
Jeg sa: "Oppe i Mishawaka. Og jeg skal fortelle deg noe. Se her." Jeg trakk frem et langt papir. "De vil at jeg skal holde vekkelsesmøter for dem over hele landet."
Hun sa: "Du?"
Og jeg sa: "Ja."
Hun sa: "Virkelig? Er det sant?"
Jeg sa: "Ja, de har fortalt meg at jeg kan holde vekkelsesmøter for dem."
Hun sa: "Vel…"
Jeg spurte: "Blir du med meg?"
Hun sa: "Selvfølgelig." Gud velsigne henne. Hun sa: "Selvfølgelig, jeg blir med." Og det er en ekte kone, som blir med deg gjennom tykt og tynt.

text:
Well, now, when I started out to hold the meetings then, I was going to go to hold them. And I went and told my mother. She said, "Well, God bless you, honey." She said, "Years ago down in Kentucky at the old Lone Star meeting house, we used to hear the people shout like that long time ago, and had that kind of a demonstration." Said, "But it faded out."
And I said, "Well, mama, these people, It ain't faded out of them." I said, "They sure have got a [unclear words]." And so she went [unclear words] things.
text_no:
Vel, da jeg startet med å holde møtene, skulle jeg gå for å arrangere dem. Jeg gikk og fortalte det til moren min. Hun sa: "Gud velsigne deg, kjære." Hun sa: "For mange år siden, nede i Kentucky ved det gamle Lone Star møtelokalet, pleide vi å høre folk juble slik for lenge siden, og hadde den typen demonstrasjon." Hun sa: "Men det har falmet bort."
Jeg sa: "Vel, mamma, hos disse folkene har det ikke falmet bort." Jeg sa: "De har virkelig fått et [uklare ord]." Og så gikk hun [uklare ord] tingene.

text:
When we got to her mother--got to her mother [unclear words]. When we got over there, why there's where the trouble started, right there.
She said, "William Branham, do you mean to tell me that you'd take my daughter out amongst a bunch of trash like that?"
I said, "Well, look, Mrs. Brumbach. They're not trash."
She said, "That's a bunch of holy-rollers." She said, "And you take her out of here, she'll starve to death." She said, "Today she might have something to eat, and tomorrow she might not have nothing to eat."
But brother, I come to find out what she called "trash" was "the cream of the crop." And bless my heart [unclear words].
And said, "You mean to tell me that you'd take…" Said…
And Hope started crying. And she said, "Mother…" She said, "I--I--I want to go with him."
And she said, "Very well, Hope. If you go, your mother will go in a grave heartbroken. That's all." And then Hope started crying.
text_no:
Da vi kom til hennes mor, begynte problemene med en gang. Hun sa, "William Branham, mener du å fortelle meg at du vil ta med datteren min blant en slik gjeng?"
Jeg svarte, "Vel, se her, fru Brumbach. De er ikke noen gjeng."
Hun sa, "Det er en gjeng hellige rullere." Og fortsatte, "Tar du henne bort, vil hun sulte i hjel. I dag har hun kanskje noe å spise, men i morgen har hun ingenting."
Men Bror, jeg fant ut at det hun kalte "søppel" var "de beste av de beste". Og, velsigne hjertet mitt.
Hun fortsatte, "Mener du virkelig å si at du vil ta henne ..."
Hope begynte å gråte og sa, "Mor, jeg—jeg—jeg vil gå med ham."
Moren svarte, "Veldig bra, Hope. Hvis du går, vil din mor gå i graven med et knust hjerte." Da begynte Hope å gråte enda mer.

text:
And there, friends, is where my sorrows started. I listened to my mother-in-law in the stead of God. He was giving me the opportunity. And there this gift would've been manifested long time ago, if I'd just went ahead and done what God told me to do.
But instead of that, I didn't want her to be angry, and I didn't want to hurt nobody's feelings. And so I just let it go like that. Just walked, I just said, "All right, we won't go."
And right there, the sorrows started. Immediately after that, my father died. My brother was killed a few nights later from that. I almost lost my own… I lost my father, my brother, my wife, my baby, and my sister-in-law, and almost my own life within about six month's time. And just started going down. My church, pretty near everything went down, down, down. Hope taken sick.
text_no:
Der, venner, startet mine sorger. Jeg lyttet til min svigermor i stedet for Gud. Han ga meg muligheten, og denne gaven ville ha blitt manifestert for lenge siden om jeg bare hadde gjort som Gud ba meg om.
Men i stedet for det, ønsket jeg ikke å gjøre henne sint, og jeg ville ikke såre noen følelser. Så jeg lot det bare gå. Jeg sa bare: "Greit, vi dropper det."
Akkurat der startet sorgene. Rett etter det døde min far. Min bror ble drept noen netter senere. Jeg mistet nesten mitt eget liv... Jeg mistet faren min, broren min, kona mi, babyen min, og min svigerinne, og nesten mitt eget liv, alt innen omtrent seks måneder. Og det begynte å gå nedover. Menigheten min, omtrent alt gikk nedover. Hope ble syk.

text:
Just right after that, the 1937 flood come on. And when it did, the… I was--got a job then. I went to working for the conservation. And I was patrolling out in the… So when I--the floods begin to come up and you remember hearing it here. Many of you was there, and how people being washed away and things.
And Hope taken sick. She was going over to get me a Christmas present. And … the Foxe's "Book Of The Martyrs," is what I wanted for a Christmas present. And she got me a little fish box.
And when I come in that afternoon, she was laying on the floor, fainted. And I called our family doctor, Doctor Adair. And he came up there, and he said, "Well, Bill, she's got pneumonia." So he said, "You have to stay up all night [unclear words] nights." And during that time…
Before that, a little girl baby, little Sharon Rose (Bless her little heart. She's in heaven too today.), she had been born into our home, just the sweetest little thing you ever seen, just a few months old.
text_no:
Rett etter det kom flommen i 1937. Da det skjedde, fikk jeg en jobb og begynte å arbeide for bevaringen. Jeg patruljerte ute da flommen begynte å stige. Mange av dere husker sikkert å ha hørt om det, og noen av dere var der— hvordan folk ble skylt bort og lignende.
Hope ble syk. Hun skulle hente en julegave til meg— Foxe's "Book of Martyrs," som jeg ønsket meg til jul. Hun kjøpte en liten fiskeeske til meg.
Da jeg kom hjem den ettermiddagen, lå hun besvimt på gulvet. Jeg ringte vår familielege, Dr. Adair. Han kom og sa: "Vel, Bill, hun har lungebetennelse. Du må være oppe hele natten i flere netter." I løpet av den tiden...
Før det, hadde lille Sharon Rose, vår nyfødte datter (Velsigne hennes lille hjerte. Hun er i himmelen nå.), kommet inn i vårt hjem. Hun var den søteste lille skapningen du noen gang har sett, bare noen få måneder gammel.

text:
And so then I remember that Doctor Adair told me, he'd say, "Have to stay up late, Billy, keep the children out of the room here." And said, "Stay up and give a lot of fluid that night." And I did.
And the next morning her mother wanted to take her down at the house. And she didn't care too much about Doctor Adair, and taken her out and throwed her into the tubercular.
So then, I remember the flood coming on; they rushed her out to the government depot, out there for the hospital. And, oh, that part of the night, it raining, twisting, blowing; and how brother, sister [unclear words] now.
Always mind God. No matter what it is, God says for you to do. And I tell you, today, that God in heaven, who looks down upon me standing in this platform, will forgive me. I know that many thousands of souls that I'll have to answer for at that day, for listening to somebody else instead of God. That's true.
Now, I remember out there that night. They taken her out to the government barracks where, used it for people who are in hospitals. And the floods were on.
text_no:
Jeg husker at doktor Adair sa til meg: "Billy, du må være oppe sent og holde barna unna rommet her." Han sa også: "Gi henne mye væske den natten." Og det gjorde jeg.
Neste morgen ønsket moren hennes å ta henne med hjem. Hun hadde ikke særlig sans for doktor Adair og tok henne ut og kastet henne praktisk talt til tuberkulosen.
Deretter kom flommen, og de fraktet henne til et statlig depot, som fungerte som sykehus. Den natten regnet, blåste og stormet det kraftig. Jeg husker hvordan Bror og søster [uklare ord].
Alltid lytt til Gud. Uansett hva det gjelder, gjør som Gud sier. Og jeg forteller dere i dag at Gud i himmelen, som ser ned på meg mens jeg står her på denne plattformen, vil tilgi meg. Jeg vet at jeg vil måtte svare for mange tusen sjeler den dagen, for å ha lyttet til andre i stedet for Gud. Det er sant.
Jeg husker ute den natten at de tok henne med til de statlige brakkene som ble brukt som sykehus. Flommen raste.

text:
And I was down trying to patrol. I slip out to see her. And she was sick, and both babies had taken pneumonia. And they were laying there sick and… And I'd worked back. They was calling me everywhere in patrol car I was in… I went downtown. And I was coming up the street along about eleven o'clock.
And the old dike had broke through up there. And down through the other part of the city it'd washed, just washed out. And they didn't know how many was killed or nothing. And such a horrible time.
And I remember I heard somebody hollering and screaming. I looked way over there past Chestnut Street, a big two story building and it was shaking like this. And there stood a mother out there with her baby in her arms, and the building going down, screaming for mercy.
text_no:
Jeg var ute på patrulje. Jeg snek meg ut for å se henne. Hun var syk, og begge babyene hadde fått lungebetennelse. De lå der syke... Jeg hadde jobbet tilbake. De ringte meg overalt i patruljebilen jeg var i... Jeg dro til sentrum. Det var rundt klokken elleve på kvelden.
Den gamle demningen hadde brutt sammen der oppe. Nedover i den andre delen av byen hadde det blitt vasket bort. De visste ikke hvor mange som var døde eller noe annet. En fryktelig tid.
Jeg husker at jeg hørte noen rope og skrike. Jeg så over mot Chestnut Street, en stor to-etasjers bygning som ristet kraftig. Der sto en mor ute med babyen i armene, og bygningen var i ferd med å falle sammen mens hun skrek om nåde.

text:
Well, I lived on the river, and I thought I was a pretty good boatman. I'd get … went and got my boat out of the back of my car, and set it in the water, the little patrol car I had. And I set the boat in the water.
And I got out there to her, and got her in and two or three other girls in the room. And I got them out. And just the time I got them to the bank, they heard… She said, "My…" She fainted. She hollered, "My baby, my baby. Get my baby." I thought she'd left her little bitty baby in the room, and I'd left it.
text_no:
Jeg bodde ved elven og trodde jeg var en dyktig båtfører. Jeg gikk for å hente båten fra bilen min og satte den ut i vannet. Jeg fikk tak i båten og satte den ut i vannet.
Jeg rodde ut til henne og hentet henne sammen med to eller tre andre jenter i rommet. Jeg fikk dem i sikkerhet på land. Akkurat da vi kom til bredden, besvimte hun og ropte: "Babyen min, babyen min. Hent babyen min." Jeg tenkte at hun hadde forlatt den lille babyen i rommet, og at jeg hadde glemt den.

text:
So I started back. And the water just a twisting; I couldn't hardly make it. And I finally got way up this way and come down, and caught a hold of the outside post and tied my boat, and went in. The baby she was talking about is the baby she had in her arms, about two and a half years old.
And then when I heard the building go out from under me… And I run out real quick, and I fell in the water, probably twenty-five feet. And I fell in the water, and just got a hold of the boat like this to pull the … keep it from pulling my boat down too. And undone the … loosed the knot in the rope, fell into the boat.
Then came freezing like that. I couldn't get the outboard motor started on it. Out into the river, I whirled right out into the main part of the current, me pulling and pulling. And it wouldn't start. Them great waves, almost as high as this building here, licking up like that, and that little bitty boat like that, and me out there [unclear words] Ohio Falls, just about a mile and a half below me there, going right to them, which meant death at any minute… And there, brother, I had to think it over, whether it was trash or not. I was going out to see.
text_no:
Så jeg begynte å gå tilbake. Vannet virvlet rundt; jeg hadde nesten ikke mulighet til å komme meg frem. Til slutt kom jeg meg langt oppover og gikk nedover, grep tak i ytterstolpen, bandt båten og gikk inn. Babyen hun snakket om var den hun holdt i armene, omtrent to og et halvt år gammel.
Da jeg hørte bygningen falle sammen under meg, løp jeg ut så raskt jeg kunne og falt sannsynligvis syv-åtte meter ned i vannet. Jeg falt i vannet, fikk tak i båten på et vis for å forhindre at den ble dratt ned også. Jeg løste opp knuten på tauet og falt ned i båten.
Så kom kulden. Jeg klarte ikke å starte påhengsmotoren. Jeg ble dratt ut i elven, rett inn i hovedstrømmen, og prøvde og prøvde, men motoren ville ikke starte. Store bølger, nesten like høye som bygningen her, slo mot meg og den lille båten var der ute. Ohio Falls lå bare drøyt to kilometer nedenfor meg, og jeg var på vei rett mot dem, noe som kunne bety døden når som helst. Der, bror, måtte jeg virkelig tenke gjennom om det var verdt det eller ikke. Jeg var på vei til å finne det ut.

text:
There, pulling on that rope, and it wouldn't start, and I'd pull again, and it wouldn't start. Sick wife and baby laying out there, just lost my daddy and everything, I knelt down in the boat, and I said, "Oh God, have mercy. Have mercy. I don't want to die out here in this river like this. And I want to raise them children. Please, dear heavenly Father, if You'll just let it start, dear God."
And that boat rocking from side to side like that, me trying to pull then. I thought, "Oh, it can't be but just a little bit farther to the falls. I knowed that was the end of all of it then, because them big waves like that, and they're coming back this way, take me right into the whirlpool there. And it's seventy or eighty feet deep right straight down through there. In normal times if anybody ever goes in there, that's all of it. And hang on those big rock ledges down through there. And there seldom ever find their body.
text_no:
Jeg dro i tauet, men motoren startet ikke. Jeg prøvde igjen, men nok en gang startet den ikke. Jeg hadde en syk kone og en baby ombord, og jeg hadde nettopp mistet faren min. Jeg knelte ned i båten og ba: "Å Gud, ha barmhjertighet. Ha barmhjertighet. Jeg vil ikke dø her ute på elven slik. Jeg vil oppdra disse barna. Vær så snill, kjære himmelske Far, hvis Du bare lar motoren starte, kjære Gud."
Båten gynget fra side til side mens jeg prøvde å starte motoren igjen. Jeg tenkte: "Det kan ikke være langt igjen til fossen." Jeg visste at det ville være slutten, for de store bølgene ville dra meg rett inn i malstrømmen der. Det er sytti eller åtti fot rett ned. Under normale forhold kommer ingen derfra med livet i behold. Kroppene henger seg fast på de store steinavsatsene, og de blir sjelden funnet igjen.

text:
And so I just praying out there, and I said, "God, I know I've done wrong. I know I oughtn't to have listened to what I did. Please, dear heavenly Father." Just trying to start. And just in a few moments, it give a couple of little sputters and started. "Oh, Lord."
Back in. I landed, come back and give it all I could, and heard … cutting this way, and praying my gasoline hold out. Finally landed way down towards New Albany there, the other corner. And got in and went back and got my boat--or got my car.
And when I got up there and found out about the mother, and everything was all right. I slipped out to the ho--or the government hospital to find out how my wife was. I was going to talk to her about it. And I went out there, and they was just laying in little old army cots.
And when I got there, it was all covered over with water. Where were they at? Then I started screaming to the top… And I got excited then.
text_no:
Jeg sto der og ba og sa: "Gud, jeg vet jeg har gjort galt. Jeg vet jeg ikke burde ha hørt på det jeg gjorde. Vær så snill, kjære himmelske Far". Jeg prøvde bare å begynne. Etter noen få øyeblikk ga det et par små sputter og startet. "Å, Herre."
Tilbake igjen. Jeg landet, kom tilbake og ga alt jeg kunne, og hørte … kuttet denne veien, og ba om at bensinen min skulle holde ut. Til slutt landet jeg langt nede mot New Albany der, på det andre hjørnet. Jeg kom meg inn, tilbake og hentet bilen min.
Da jeg kom frem og fant ut om moren, var alt i orden. Jeg snek meg ut til det statlige sykehuset for å finne ut hvordan det sto til med min kone. Jeg skulle snakke med henne om det. Da jeg kom dit, lå de på små gamle feltsenger.
Og da jeg kom dit, var alt oversvømt med vann. Hvor var de? Jeg begynte å skrike av full hals … Jeg ble veldig opprørt da.

text:
Major Weekly, a friend of mine there at the government, he walked up to me. He said, "Reverend Branham?"
I said, "Yes, sir."
He said, "I don't think your wife is gone." Said, "I think they got everybody out of there." Said, "I think they went to Charlestown, a city about twelve, fourteen miles above here." Said, "I think they went out in a cattle car." Her with pneumonia and it sleeting and blowing like that. Two sick babies and them with pneumonia, one of them just eight months old. I thought, "Oh mercy, they was on a cattle car."
Then I jumped in my truck and run out there towards--to get the road to go to Charlestown. There's about six miles of water where the Lancassange Creek had come through like this to get back. I run down and got my speed boat, and I'd tried my best to get through them waves. I'd hit like this and go plumb back around like that, and I tried to duck the waves.
text_no:
Major Weekly, en venn av meg i regjeringen, kom bort til meg og sa: "Predikant Branham?"
Jeg svarte: "Ja, sir."
Han sa: "Jeg tror ikke din kone er borte. Jeg tror de fikk alle ut derfra. Jeg tror de dro til Charlestown, en by omtrent tolv til fjorten kilometer herfra. Jeg tror de dro i en storfekjerre." Min kone med lungebetennelse, i et slikt vær med sludd og sterk vind. To syke babyer, begge med lungebetennelse, og en av dem bare åtte måneder gammel. Jeg tenkte: "Å, Herre, de var på en storfekjerre."
Jeg hoppet inn i lastebilen og kjørte mot veien til Charlestown. Rundt seks kilometer med vann dekket veien på grunn av Lancassange Creek som hadde flommet over. Jeg løp ned og tok motorbåten min, og gjorde mitt beste for å navigere gjennom bølgene. Båten slo i bølgene og snudde rundt, og jeg prøvde å unngå de verste kastene.

text:
And out there, I got cut off from everything, out there myself. And I set out there marooned for about eight days where they had to drop me something to eat. I had a lot of time to think over on who I was going to listen to, God or somebody else, let it be one who loves his mother or whoever it was. You listen to what God's got to tell you [unclear words].
I'd set there and I prayed and cried… [Blank spot on tape.] Got to make your stand. And then instead of standing up against it, I thought more of some--what some woman respected than what my own conscience and God was planting in my heart. I said, "Oh, God, what can I do?"
I looked down there and I met another fellow, and I fixing [unclear words] "Did any of them in the hospital get drowned, you know?"
He said, "No, I don't think there was." He said, "I think they all escaped." And said, "Reverend Branham, I think your wife was on a box car, and they took her out to Charlestown when that boat went up."
text_no:
Der ute ble jeg kuttet av fra alt, helt alene. Jeg satt fast der ute i omtrent åtte dager, og de måtte slippe ned mat til meg. Jeg hadde god tid til å tenke over hvem jeg skulle lytte til: Gud eller noen andre, enten det var en som elsket sin mor eller hvem det måtte være. Du må lytte til hva Gud har å fortelle deg.
Jeg satt der og ba og gråt... [Tomt.område.på.lydbånd.] Du må ta et standpunkt. I stedet for å stå imot, tenkte jeg mer på respekt fra en eller annen kvinne enn på hva min egen samvittighet og Gud plantet i mitt hjerte. Jeg sa: "Å, Gud, hva kan jeg gjøre?"
Jeg så ned og møtte en annen mann. Jeg spurte, "Ble noen av dem på sykehuset druknet?"
Han svarte: "Nei, jeg tror ikke det. Jeg tror de alle slapp unna." Og han fortsatte: "Bror Branham, jeg tror din kone var på en godsvogn, og de tok henne ut til Charlestown når båten gikk opp."

text:
Well, I run down to my car and got my speedboat, and come back up and put it on the back of my truck. Run up and started to cross. I set up there to [unclear words] was back to it, and about six miles of water just waves through there.
Some of them said, "That train?" Said "It washed off the trestles right up there." Oh, my. There it was again. I tell you, brother, back down in behind this heart, there is sorrows that you know nothing about…
Then I put that boat in the water, and I tried hour after hour to pierce that current. And I couldn't do it. And then the water cut me off, and there I was marooned out there for about seven days I set out there. I had plenty of time to think things over. When the waters got down [unclear words] I had two choices. I walked up there [unclear words] [Blank spot on tape.]
text_no:
Jeg løp ned til bilen min og hentet speedbåten, kom tilbake og satte den på baksiden av lastebilen. Jeg kjørte dit og begynte å krysse vannet. Det var omtrent seks mil med bølger gjennom området.
Noen sa: "Ser du toget?" De sa: "Det har blitt vasket bort fra broen der oppe." Å, herregud. Der var det igjen. Jeg forteller deg, bror, dypt inne i hjertet er det sorger du ikke vet noe om...
Så satte jeg båten på vannet og prøvde i flere timer å skjære gjennom strømmen. Jeg klarte det ikke. Vannet kuttet meg av, og jeg ble strandet der i omtrent syv dager. Jeg hadde rikelig med tid til å tenke. Da vannet trakk seg tilbake, hadde jeg to valg. Jeg gikk opp der [uklare ord] [Tomt.område.på.lydbånd].

text:
They had on boots. I was going just as hard as I could. I run up to an old friend of mine, Mr. Hayes, called him Colonel Hayes, superintendent of the public service company. I went up to him, and I said, "Mr. Hayes." I said, "Did that train come through with that bunch of people fr…"
He said, "I don't know, Billy."
I said, "Let's go." We went on down through… Just a little bitty city there, about two or three thousand people. We went everywhere. Nobody had heard anything of my wife.
Oh, I thought, "Wife and babies are wound around some bailing wire or something or other down there in one of them swamps, and maybe plumb down in the south somewhere; swelled up, laying there in a bunch of bushes, drowned."
"Oh God," I said, "what can I do? What can I do?"
text_no:
De hadde på seg støvler. Jeg gikk så fort jeg kunne. Jeg løp opp til en gammel venn av meg, Mr. Hayes, vi kalte ham Oberst Hayes, overordnet for offentlig tjenesteforsyning. Jeg gikk bort til ham og sa: "Mr. Hayes, kom den toget gjennom med den mengden mennesker?"
Han svarte: "Jeg vet ikke, Billy."
Jeg sa: "La oss gå." Vi gikk gjennom... Det var bare en liten by der, med rundt to eller tre tusen mennesker. Vi gikk alle steder. Ingen hadde hørt noe om min kone.
Åå, jeg tenkte: "Kone og barna er kanskje viklet inn i noen ståltråd eller noe annet der nede i en av disse sumpene, og kanskje langt sør; oppsvulmet, liggende der i en haug med busker, druknet."
"Å Gud," sa jeg, "hva kan jeg gjøre? Hva kan jeg gjøre?"

text:
I went out. We went down to the railroad station. And there's a dispatcher there said, "Just a minute." Said, "I believe that train come through." Said, "The engineer of that train is here in the city today." Said, "He was supposed to be down here in a little bit to take a train out." Said, "I'll ask him." After while, he… I run to him as soon as they told me that was him coming."
I said, "Sir, did you drive the train from the government depot that come through?"
He said, "Yes, I drove that train."
I said, "Do you know Charlie Brumbach?"
Said, "Certainly." He said, "His daughter was on the train on back there with two sick babies."
And I said, "That's my wife, sir." I said, "Where're they at?"
Said, "They're--they're somewhere. I let them off at, I believe at--at the Kokomo, Indiana."
And I said, "You did?"
He said, "Yes."
text_no:
Jeg gikk ut. Vi dro ned til jernbanestasjonen. Der sa en trafikkontrollør: "Et øyeblikk. Jeg tror toget har passert." Han fortsatte: "Lokomotivføreren på dette toget er i byen i dag. Han skal etter planen komme hit snart for å ta ut et tog." Så sa han: "Jeg skal spørre ham." Etter en stund kom lokomotivføreren, og jeg løp bort til ham så snart de fortalte at det var ham.
Jeg spurte: "Unnskyld, kjørte du toget fra regjeringsdepotet som kom forbi?"
Han svarte: "Ja, jeg kjørte det toget."
Jeg spurte: "Kjenner du Charlie Brumbach?"
Han sa: "Selvfølgelig. Hans datter var på toget bak der med to syke barn."
Jeg sa: "Det er min kone, sir. Hvor er de nå?"
Han svarte: "De er et sted. Jeg satte dem av, jeg tror det var i Kokomo, Indiana."
Jeg sa: "Gjorde du det?"
Han svarte: "Ja."

text:
And I started at foot to walk. Could… I was going to get there some way. I begin to come almost beside myself. I started off. I met a man. He said, "I know who you're looking for, Billy." It was a friend of mine. Said, "You're looking for Hope, aren't you."
And I said, "Jim, you know about her?"
Said… I mean at … not at Kokomo, it's Seymour, Indiana.
He said, "She's laying up there in the Baptist church at Seymour, Indiana, dying with tuberculosis, laying by the side of my wife." And I … or "my girlfriend."
And I said, "Dying with TB?"
Said, "Yes, Bill." Said, "I hate to tell you, but you wouldn't know her."
I said, "Is the babies alive?"
Said, "I don't know nothing about the babies."
Oh, my. I said, "Oh, can we get there?"
Said, "I got a secret road." Said, "I can take you."
And we got in there late that night, in the basketball arena where the Baptist church was fixed up for the refugees to come in. And they said she was down there. And I run through there screaming top of my voice, "Hope! Hope, honey! Where are you? Where are you?" And I looked.
text_no:
Jeg begynte å gå mot målet. På en eller annen måte skulle jeg klare å komme dit. Jeg begynte å miste fatningen. På veien møtte jeg en mann. Han sa, "Jeg vet hvem du leter etter, Billy." Det var en venn av meg. Han sa, "Du leter etter Hope, ikke sant?"
Jeg svarte, "Jim, vet du noe om henne?"
Han sa, "Hun ligger oppe i den baptistkirken i Seymour, Indiana, og dør av tuberkulose, ved siden av kjæresten min."
Jeg spurte, "Døde med tuberkulose?"
"Ja, Bill," svarte han. "Jeg hater å fortelle deg dette, men du ville ikke kjent henne igjen."
Jeg spurte, "Er barna i live?"
Han svarte, "Jeg vet ingenting om barna."
Å, Gud. Jeg sa, "Kan vi komme dit?"
Han sa, "Jeg vet om en hemmelig vei. Jeg kan ta deg med."
Vi ankom sent den natten, til basketballarenaen hvor baptistkirken var gjort klar for flyktninger. De fortalte oss at hun var der. Jeg løp gjennom hallen og ropte av full hals, "Hope! Hope, kjære! Hvor er du? Hvor er du?" Og jeg så meg rundt.

text:
Oh, I will never forget that. Back over there on this old government cot, I seen a little bony hand raise up. That was my darling. I run to her real quick. I fell down at her. Those dark eyes was sunk way back in her head. She'd falling lots of weight.
I said, "Sweetheart?"
She said, "I look awful, don't I?"
I said, "No, honey. Is the babies all right?"
"Yes," she said. "Mother has the babies." Said, "Billy's been awfully sick; Sharon's a little better." And she said, "I'm awfully sick."
I started crying, I said, "God, don't take her from me. Please don't, Lord."
text_no:
Jeg vil aldri glemme det. Tilbake der, på den gamle feltsengen, så jeg en liten beinete hånd løfte seg. Det var min kjære. Jeg løp raskt til henne og falt ned ved hennes side. De mørke øynene hennes var sunket dypt inn i hodet. Hun hadde tapt mye vekt.
Jeg sa, "Kjære?"
Hun svarte, "Jeg ser forferdelig ut, gjør jeg ikke?"
Jeg sa, "Nei, kjære. Er barna ok?"
"Ja," sa hun. "Mor har barna. Billy har vært veldig syk; Sharon er litt bedre." Og hun sa, "Jeg er fryktelig syk."
Jeg begynte å gråte og sa, "Gud, ikke ta henne fra meg. Vær så snill, Herre."

text:
I felt somebody touch me on the back. It was a doctor. "You're Reverend Branham?"
And I said, "Yes, sir."
"Come here just a minute." Said, "Aren't you a friend of Sam Adair?"
And I said, "Yes, sir, I am."
He said, "I hate to tell you this, Reverend Branham, but your wife's a dying." Said, "Your wife's got tubercular. Sam told me to tell you just to make her comfortable, and not to be excited around her."
I said, "She dying, doctor?" I said, "She can't, doctor. That's all. She can't do it." I said, "I love her with all my heart, and I'm a Christian." And I said, "I just--I just know she ain't going to die. I just can't think of the thoughts to think that she'd be taken away from me here, and with these two little babies, how could I stand it?"
He said, "Well, I hate to tell you, but," said, "there's nothing can be done as far as I know."
text_no:
Jeg følte noen berøre meg på ryggen. Det var en lege. "Er du pastor Branham?" spurte han.
"Ja, det er jeg," svarte jeg.
"Kan jeg snakke med deg et øyeblikk? Er du venn med Sam Adair?"
"Ja, det stemmer," sa jeg.
"Jeg beklager å måtte fortelle deg dette, pastor Branham, men din kone er døende. Hun har tuberkulose. Sam ba meg fortelle deg at du bare skal gjøre henne komfortabel og ikke skape stress rundt henne."
"Er hun døende, doktor?" svarte jeg. "Det kan ikke være sant, hun kan ikke dø." Jeg fortsatte, "Jeg elsker henne av hele mitt hjerte, og jeg er en kristen. Jeg nekter å tro at hun skal bli tatt bort fra meg, her med våre to små barn. Hvordan skulle jeg klare det?"
Legen svarte, "Jeg beklager, men det finnes ingen behandling som jeg kjenner til."

text:
I went back to her, trying to brace myself up and talk to her. A few days we took her home. She just kept getting worse and worse and worse. Went to Louisville, and they had specialists and everything. Took her out to the hospital. Doctor Miller from the sanatorium come down and looked. He called me out to one side, said, "Reverend Branham, she's going to die." Said, "There ain't nothing can be done for her." Said, "She's--she's going to die."
I said, "Doctor Miller, honest, isn't there something I can do? Could I take her to Arizona? Could I do something for her?"
Said, "It's too late now, Billy." Said, "That--that's a… That's galloping tubercular." Said, "It--it kills them right away." Said, "Her family's had it back behind there," which I knew later that they did. And said, "She's just broke with it, and it's got such a hold on her." Said he'd give her pneumothorax treatments and everything. And said…
text_no:
Jeg gikk tilbake til henne og prøvde å samle mot til å snakke med henne. Etter noen dager tok vi henne hjem. Hun ble bare dårligere og dårligere. Vi dro til Louisville, hvor spesialister undersøkte henne. Til slutt ble hun innlagt på sykehuset. Doktor Miller fra sanatoriet kom ned og undersøkte henne. Han tok meg til side og sa: "Pastor Branham, hun kommer til å dø. Det er ingenting som kan gjøres for henne. Hun er ferdig."
Jeg svarte: "Doktor Miller, vær så ærlig, er det virkelig ikke noe jeg kan gjøre? Kan jeg ta henne til Arizona eller noe annet?"
Han sa: "Det er for sent nå, Billy. Det er galopperende tuberkulose. Det tar livet av dem raskt. Familien hennes har hatt det før," noe jeg senere bekreftet. Han sa videre at hun akkurat hadde fått det og at det hadde et så sterkt grep om henne. Han hadde gitt henne pneumothorax-behandlinger og alt annet som var mulig.

text:
And I'd hold her hand when they were boring that hole in her side to collapse them lungs. If I had it to go over, it wouldn't be done. And she'd hold my hand there, bless her heart. I'd have to almost pull her hand off of mine from suffering, holding where they'd bore that hole in there and collapse the lungs on the side. And that was tubercular traveling right on up like that. I knew she was going, and I was doing all that I could do.
And I'd work at night. I remember, I was out, and I heard a patrol sign come through. It said, "Calling William Branham. Come to the hospital immediately, wife dying."
text_no:
Jeg holdt hennes hånd mens de boret et hull i siden hennes for å kollapse lungene. Hvis jeg kunne gjøre det om igjen, ville det ikke ha blitt gjort. Hun holdt min hånd, stakkars henne. Jeg måtte nesten rive hånden hennes av min på grunn av smerten hun led, mens de boret det hullet og kollapset lungene på siden. Tuberkulosen spredte seg raskt. Jeg visste at hun kom til å dø, og jeg gjorde alt jeg kunne.
Jeg jobbet om natten. Jeg husker at jeg var ute, og jeg hørte en patruljemelding. Den sa: "William Branham, kom til sykehuset umiddelbart, kona din holder på å dø."

text:
I never will forget; I took off my hat. Setting in the truck, I held up my hands, and I said, "O Jesus, please don't let her go. Let me talk to her once more before she goes. Please do save her." I was about twenty miles away from home. I turned on lights and everything. I went down the road real swift, stopped in front of the hospital, and throwed off the gun belt, and into the place I went real quick. I started walking down through the Clark County Memorial Hospital.
As I started down through there, I looked, and I seen poor little Doctor Adair come walking down through there with his head down. God bless that man. And he looked at me like that when he seen me. He throwed his hands up like that and started crying and run in the halls. And I run up to him, put my arm around him, I said, "Sam, is it?"
And he said, "Billy, I'm--I'm afraid she's gone now."
I said, "Come, go with me, Doc. Let's go in."
